returned_sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
If your employer has not paid your holiday pay, you should first check your employment contract and company policies to confirm your entitlement. Holiday pay is a legal right, and you should be paid for any holiday days you have taken. If you work irregular hours or part-time, your holiday pay should be calculated fairly and not included as "rolled-up holiday pay" in your hourly rate if your leave year started after 31 March 2024.

You should talk to your employer to explain the issue and provide any evidence you have, such as your contract or records of holiday taken. If this does not resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer. If your employer still does not pay what you are owed, you may be able to take your case to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction from wages.

Step-by-step suggestion:
1. Check your employment contract and any company holiday policies to confirm your holiday pay entitlement.
2. Gather evidence such as payslips, holiday records, and your contract.
3. Speak to your employer or payroll department to raise the issue informally.
4. If unresolved, raise a formal grievance following your employer’s grievance procedure.
5. If the problem persists, consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice and possibly making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

answer:
If your employer has not paid you for holiday days you have taken, you should first check your employment contract and company policies to confirm your holiday pay entitlement. Holiday pay is a legal right, and your employer must pay you for the holidays you have taken. If you believe you are owed holiday pay, try to resolve the issue by talking to your employer and showing any evidence you have, such as your contract or payslips.

If talking to your employer does not work, you can raise a formal grievance with them. If the problem remains unresolved, you may be able to take your case to an employment tribunal for unpaid holiday pay. It’s also a good idea to keep records of your communications and any evidence of your entitlement.

Step-by-step suggestion:
1. Check your contract and any company holiday policies to confirm your holiday pay rights.
2. Talk to your employer and explain the issue, providing any evidence you have.
3. If needed, raise a formal grievance following your employer’s grievance procedure.
4. If the issue is still not resolved, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about taking legal action.

returned_sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
If you work irregular hours, your holiday pay is calculated based on your average pay over a certain period, usually the last 52 weeks in which you were paid. This means your employer will look at how much you earned and how many hours you worked during those weeks to work out an average hourly or weekly rate. Your holiday pay should then reflect this average rate, so you are paid fairly for the time you take off.

For irregular-hours workers, all holiday leave must be paid at your "normal" rate of pay, which includes things like commission and regular overtime payments, but usually not bonuses. This ensures that your holiday pay matches what you would typically earn if you were working.

Step-by-step suggestion:
1. Your employer will gather your pay and hours worked for the last 52 weeks where you received pay.
2. They calculate your average hourly pay by dividing total pay by total hours worked.
3. They multiply this average hourly rate by the number of hours of holiday you take.
4. This gives your holiday pay for the time off.

If you have worked less than 52 weeks, the calculation will be based on the weeks you have worked.

- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

answer:
1) Your employer generally cannot include holiday pay in your hourly rate, a practice known as "rolled-up holiday pay." This means your holiday pay should be paid separately from your normal wages. However, there is an exception for workers with irregular hours or part-year workers: rolled-up holiday pay can be used for these workers unless their leave year started on or before 31 March 2024.

2) If you think your employer is including holiday pay in your hourly rate and you are not an irregular-hours or part-year worker (or your leave year started after 31 March 2024), you can:
- Check your contract and payslips to see how holiday pay is calculated and paid.
- Contact Acas for guidance or raise the issue with your employer.
- If you believe you are owed holiday pay, gather evidence such as your contract and payslips and seek advice from Acas or Citizens Advice.

What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
If your employer hasn’t paid your wages on payday, you should first talk to them to find out why. It might be a mistake they can fix quickly. If you have a payslip, check it to understand what’s missing or wrong. If talking to your employer doesn’t resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance with them if they have a grievance procedure. Keep working while you try to sort this out, as refusing to work could risk your job.

If your employer still doesn’t pay you what you’re owed, you may be able to take legal action. You can make a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages, but you must do this within 3 months minus one day from when you should have been paid. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim through a small claims court instead. It’s often helpful to get advice from a trade union or an employment adviser before taking legal steps.

Step-by-step suggestion:
1. Check your payslip and employment contract to confirm what you should be paid.
2. Talk to your employer or payroll department to ask why you haven’t been paid.
3. If the problem isn’t resolved, raise a formal grievance with your employer if possible.
4. Keep working while you try to resolve the issue.
5. If unpaid wages continue, consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limit.
6. If you miss the tribunal deadline, you can consider a small claims court claim.
7. Seek advice from a trade union, ACAS, or Citizens Advice for support.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

answer:
If your employer has deducted money from your wages without explaining why, you should first check your employment contract or any written agreements to see if they are allowed to make such deductions. Employers can only deduct money for specific reasons, such as overpayments or damage to company property, if this is agreed in writing or stated in your contract. They must also follow certain rules, like not deducting more than 10% of your pay before tax on each payday for some types of deductions, and providing written details of each deduction.

If you don’t think the deduction is fair or allowed, you should talk to your employer to ask for an explanation and any evidence supporting the deduction. If this doesn’t resolve the issue, you could raise a formal grievance with your employer or seek advice from an employment adviser or trade union representative.

Step-by-step suggestion:
1. Review your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.
2. Ask your employer to explain the deduction and provide written details.
3. If you disagree, try to resolve the issue informally or raise a formal grievance.
4. Seek advice from a trade union, Citizens Advice, or an employment adviser if needed.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

answer:
If you think you are being paid below the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage, you should first check the current minimum wage rates for your age group to confirm. If your pay is indeed below the legal minimum, you should talk to your employer to explain the issue and show any evidence you have, such as payslips or your contract. Sometimes this can be a mistake that your employer can fix quickly by paying you what you are owed.

If talking to your employer doesn’t resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with them if they have a grievance procedure. If that still doesn’t work, you may be able to take legal action through an employment tribunal to claim unlawful deduction from wages. Keep in mind there is a time limit of 3 months minus one day from when you should have been paid to start a tribunal claim. You can also check GOV.UK for the current minimum wage rates and get advice from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step-by-step suggestion:
1. Check the current minimum wage rates for your age on GOV.UK or ACAS.
2. Gather evidence such as payslips, your contract, and any communications about pay.
3. Talk to your employer to raise the issue informally.
4. If unresolved, raise a formal grievance with your employer.
5. If still unresolved, consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limit.
6. Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a trade union if you are a member.

answer:
If you were dismissed without a meeting, it could potentially be unfair dismissal, especially if your employer did not follow a fair and reasonable procedure. Employers are generally expected to investigate the situation fully and give you a chance to respond before dismissing you. This usually involves holding a meeting to discuss the reasons for dismissal. Not having a meeting might mean the employer did not act reasonably or fairly.

You can ask your employer for a written explanation of why you were dismissed. If you have been employed for at least 2 years, you have the right to request this in writing within 14 days. If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you may be able to appeal the decision or make a claim to an employment tribunal. It’s important to act promptly, as there are time limits for making claims.

Step-by-step suggestion:
1. Request a written statement from your employer explaining the reasons for your dismissal.
2. If you have not already, ask if you can appeal the dismissal.
3. Consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your situation.
4. If you think your dismissal was unfair and you have been employed for 2 years or more, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal. Remember to act within the time limits.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals  
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

answer:
If your employer dismissed you and refused to let you appeal, this could be a problem because employers are expected to follow a fair and reasonable procedure when dismissing someone. Usually, you should be given the chance to appeal the dismissal, and your employer should explain the reasons for the dismissal clearly. If they do not allow you to appeal, it might mean the dismissal process was unfair, which could be important if you want to challenge the dismissal.

Here’s what you could consider doing:
1. Ask your employer in writing for the reasons for your dismissal if you haven’t received them yet. If you have been employed for at least 2 years, they must provide this within 14 days.
2. Explain that you want to appeal the dismissal and ask if they will reconsider allowing an appeal.
3. If your employer still refuses, you might want to get advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about whether you have grounds to make an unfair dismissal claim.
4. Keep records of all communications with your employer about the dismissal and appeal.

Remember, this is general information and not formal legal advice. For advice specific to your situation, you should contact ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor.

answer:
If you have been treated unfairly at work because of your religion, this is considered discrimination and is against the law under the Equality Act 2010. Your religion is a protected characteristic, so you have the right to work without facing discrimination, harassment, or victimisation related to your beliefs. Your employer must take complaints seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination at work.

If you experience unfair treatment due to your religion, you can raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint or grievance procedure. Your employer should investigate and address the problem fairly. If the issue is not resolved, you may be able to take further action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step-by-step suggestion:
1. Keep a record of what happened, including dates, times, and details of the unfair treatment.
2. Talk to your employer or HR department about the issue, explaining how you have been treated unfairly because of your religion.
3. If informal talks don’t work, use your employer’s formal grievance procedure to make a complaint.
4. If your employer does not resolve the issue, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about your options, including a possible employment tribunal claim.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

answer:
Harassment at work can be a form of discrimination if it relates to a protected characteristic such as age, gender, race, disability, or other factors covered by the Equality Act 2010. Harassment means unwanted behaviour that violates your dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment for you. If your manager’s behaviour fits this description and is linked to one of these protected characteristics, it is likely harassment under the law.

If you believe you are being harassed, you can raise the issue with your employer. Many employers have policies and procedures to handle harassment complaints, and they should take your concerns seriously and investigate them properly. You can choose to raise the issue informally at first or make a formal complaint if needed. It’s important to keep a record of incidents, including dates, times, what was said or done, and any witnesses.

Step-by-step suggestion:
1. Document the harassment incidents with as much detail as possible.
2. Check if your employer has a harassment or grievance policy and follow the procedure outlined.
3. Consider raising the issue informally with your manager or HR department.
4. If informal steps don’t work or you feel unable to raise it informally, make a formal complaint in writing.
5. If the employer does not resolve the issue, you may seek advice from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice for further support.

returned_sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you work irregular hours, your holiday pay is calculated based on your average pay over a reference period. Typically, this involves working out your average hourly or weekly pay over the last 52 weeks in which you were paid. If you have fewer than 52 weeks of pay, the average is based on the weeks you have worked. This average pay includes regular overtime and commission but usually excludes bonuses. For irregular-hours workers, all holiday leave must be paid at their 'normal' rate of pay, which reflects this average.

Employers cannot include holiday pay within your hourly rate (known as 'rolled-up holiday pay') for regular-hours workers, but there are some exceptions for irregular-hours or part-year workers depending on when their leave year began. If you want to calculate your holiday pay, you can use the government’s holiday pay calculator or contact Acas for guidance.

Step 1: Calculate your average hourly or weekly pay over the last 52 weeks (or fewer if you have less service).  
Step 2: Multiply this average by the number of hours you would normally work during your holiday.  
Step 3: Confirm with your employer how they calculate your holiday pay and check your payslip for clarity.

Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers generally cannot include holiday pay in your hourly rate, a practice known as ‘rolled-up holiday pay’. This means your holiday pay should be paid separately from your normal wages. However, there is an exception for workers with irregular hours or part-year workers: employers can use rolled-up holiday pay for these workers unless their leave year began on or before 31 March 2024.

If you work irregular hours or part-year, your holiday pay should be calculated based on the hours you have worked rather than being included in your hourly rate. For more detailed guidance or if you have questions about your specific situation, you can contact ACAS.

Step 1: Check if you are a worker with irregular hours or a part-year worker.  
Step 2: Confirm when your leave year started (before or after 31 March 2024).  
Step 3: If you believe your holiday pay is being incorrectly included in your hourly rate, contact ACAS for advice.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer hasn’t paid your wages on the agreed payday, you should first talk to them to find out why. It might be a mistake they can fix quickly. If talking to your employer doesn’t resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance if your employer has a grievance procedure. Keep any evidence such as payslips or contracts that show what you are owed.

If the problem continues and your employer still hasn’t paid you, you may be able to take legal action. You can make a claim to an employment tribunal for unpaid wages, but you must do this within 3 months minus one day from the date the wages were due. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim through a small claims court instead. It’s usually best to try to resolve the issue without legal action if you plan to keep working for your employer.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department about the missing wages.  
Step 2: Check your payslip and contract to confirm what you are owed.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if necessary.  
Step 4: Consider legal action such as an employment tribunal claim or small claims court if the issue remains unresolved.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can only deduct money from your wages if it is allowed by your contract or if you have agreed in writing. For example, deductions can be made for damaged property or missing money from the till, but only if there is a written agreement and certain conditions are met, such as not deducting more than 10% of your pay before tax on each payday and providing written details of the deduction each time. If your employer has deducted money without explaining why or without your agreement, this may be unlawful.

You should first check your employment contract and any written agreements to see if such deductions are permitted. If you don’t think the deduction is lawful or you haven’t been given an explanation, you should raise the issue with your employer and ask for clarification and evidence. If this does not resolve the problem, you can seek advice from an employment adviser or organisation such as Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Review your contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Ask your employer to explain the deduction and provide written details.  
Step 3: If unresolved, seek advice from Citizens Advice or a trade union representative.  
Step 4: Consider raising a formal grievance if your employer does not respond properly.

What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe you are being paid below the National Minimum Wage (NMW) or National Living Wage (NLW), you should first check the current rates for your age group and status (for example, apprentice or worker). Employers must pay at least the legal minimum wage for the hours you work. If you find your pay is below this, you should talk to your employer to raise the issue and show any evidence you have, such as payslips or your contract.

If talking to your employer does not resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with them. If the issue remains unresolved, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. Keep in mind there is a time limit of 3 months minus one day from when the underpayment occurred to start tribunal proceedings. You can also seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for support.

Step 1: Check the current minimum wage rates for your age and status on GOV.UK or ACAS.
Step 2: Gather evidence such as payslips, contracts, and records of hours worked.
Step 3: Talk to your employer about the issue and ask for the correct pay.
Step 4: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance with your employer.
Step 5: Consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.
Step 6: If necessary, make a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limit.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Being dismissed without a meeting can be a sign that your employer did not follow a fair procedure. For a dismissal to be fair, your employer should have a valid reason and act reasonably, which usually includes investigating the situation and giving you a chance to respond, often through a meeting. If you were dismissed without any meeting or opportunity to explain your side, this could mean the dismissal was unfair, especially if you have worked for your employer for at least two years.

You have the right to ask your employer for a written statement explaining why you were dismissed. If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you can appeal the decision with your employer. If the employer does not follow a fair and reasonable procedure, you might be able to make a claim for unfair dismissal to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you have been employed for two years or more.  
Step 2: Consider appealing the dismissal if you have not already done so.  
Step 3: If you believe the dismissal was unfair and the appeal is unsuccessful, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal, but it is advisable to seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice first.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has dismissed you and refused to allow you to appeal, this may mean they have not followed a fair and reasonable procedure. Employers are generally expected to give employees the right to appeal a dismissal decision. Not allowing an appeal could be considered unfair, especially if the dismissal process did not follow the Acas Code of Practice on disciplinary and grievance procedures. If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal.

It is important to check whether your employer gave you a valid reason for dismissal and followed a consistent and reasonable process. You also have the right to ask for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal if you have been employed for at least two years (or one year if you started before April 2012). If you were dismissed while on statutory maternity leave, your employer must provide this statement regardless of your length of service.

Step 1: Request in writing the reasons for your dismissal if you have not already received them.  
Step 2: Check if your employer followed a fair procedure, including whether you were given a chance to appeal.  
Step 3: Consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your situation and the possibility of making an unfair dismissal claim.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal for an employer or anyone else to discriminate against you because of your religion or belief. This protection covers you at work, meaning your employer must not treat you unfairly, harass you, or victimise you because of your religion. You are also protected if you are associated with someone who has a protected characteristic or if you have complained about discrimination.

If you believe you have been discriminated against at work because of your religion, you can raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint or grievance procedure. Employers are required to take such complaints seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination and harassment in the workplace.

Step 1: Keep a record of incidents where you believe you were treated unfairly because of your religion.  
Step 2: Consider raising the issue informally with your employer or HR department.  
Step 3: If the problem continues, use your employer’s formal grievance procedure to make a complaint.  
Step 4: If the issue is not resolved, you may be able to take your case to an employment tribunal.

It can be considered a form of discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 if it relates to characteristics such as age, sex, race, disability, religion, sexual orientation, or others protected by law. returned_sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer hasn’t paid your wages on the agreed payday, you should first talk to them or the payroll department to find out why. It might be a mistake that they can fix quickly. You should continue working while you try to resolve the issue, as refusing to work could breach your contract and risk dismissal. If talking to your employer doesn’t solve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance if your employer has a procedure for this.

If the problem persists, you may be able to take legal action. You can make a claim to an employment tribunal for unpaid wages, but you must do this within 3 months minus one day from the date the wages were due. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim unpaid wages through a small claims court instead. Gathering evidence such as payslips and your employment contract will help support your case.

Step 1: Speak to your employer or payroll department to ask why you haven’t been paid.  
Step 2: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance with your employer.  
Step 3: If still unpaid, consider seeking advice and possibly making a claim to an employment tribunal or small claims court within the time limits.

returned_sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can only deduct money from your wages if it is allowed by your contract or if you have agreed in writing. For example, deductions can be made for things like damaged property or missing money from the till, but only if there is a written agreement permitting this. Additionally, if deductions are made for missing money or damaged stock, your employer must follow extra rules: they can only deduct up to 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, must start deductions within 12 months of noticing the issue, and must provide you with written details of each deduction.

If your employer has deducted money without explaining why or without your agreement, you should first check your contract and any written agreements to see if such deductions are allowed. If you are unsure or believe the deduction is unfair, you should seek advice from an employment adviser or organisation such as Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Ask your employer to explain the deduction and provide written details.  
Step 3: If you disagree or don’t get a satisfactory explanation, seek advice from an employment adviser or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe you are being paid below the National Minimum Wage (NMW) or National Living Wage (NLW), you should first check the current rates for your age group or apprenticeship status on the GOV.UK website or through ACAS. Employers are legally required to pay at least the minimum wage for the hours you work. If your pay is below this, your employer may be breaking the law.

You should talk to your employer to raise the issue and show any evidence you have, such as payslips or your employment contract. If this does not resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer. If the issue remains unresolved, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction from wages. Keep in mind there is a time limit of 3 months minus one day from when you should have been paid to start tribunal proceedings.

Step 1: Check the current minimum wage rates for your age and status.  
Step 2: Gather evidence like payslips and your contract.  
Step 3: Talk to your employer about the issue.  
Step 4: Raise a formal grievance if necessary.  
Step 5: Consider legal action such as an employment tribunal claim if the problem is not resolved.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Being dismissed without a meeting can be unfair if your employer did not follow a fair and reasonable procedure before ending your employment. Employers are generally expected to investigate the situation fully and give you a chance to respond, such as through a meeting or disciplinary hearing, before dismissing you. This helps ensure the dismissal is fair and justified. If your employer dismissed you without any discussion or meeting, this could be a breach of the Acas Code of Practice on disciplinary procedures, which tribunals consider when deciding if a dismissal was unfair.

However, whether the dismissal is unfair depends on the specific circumstances, including the reason for dismissal and whether your employer acted reasonably and consistently. If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you can appeal the decision with your employer or potentially make a claim to an employment tribunal, especially if you have been employed for at least two years.

Step 1: Ask your employer for the reasons for your dismissal in writing if you have not received this already.  
Step 2: Consider whether your employer followed a fair procedure, including giving you a chance to explain your side.  
Step 3: If you think the dismissal was unfair, you can appeal internally or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a tribunal claim.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals  
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has dismissed you but refused to allow you to appeal the decision, this could be a concern because employers are generally expected to follow a fair and reasonable procedure when dismissing someone. According to UK employment law and Acas guidance, a fair dismissal process usually includes giving the employee the right to appeal the dismissal. Not allowing an appeal might mean the employer has not followed a fair procedure, which could be relevant if you decide to challenge the dismissal as unfair.

You have the right to ask your employer for a written explanation of why you were dismissed, especially if you have been employed for at least two years or are on statutory maternity leave. If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal. It is advisable to raise the issue with your employer first, asking for a chance to appeal or for further explanation.

Step 2: Ask your employer if you can appeal the dismissal decision.  
Step 3: If your employer refuses and you believe the dismissal was unfair, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about making a claim to an employment tribunal.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal for an employer to discriminate against you because of your religion or belief. This protection applies to all aspects of work, including hiring, pay, promotion, and dismissal. You are also protected if you are associated with someone who has a protected characteristic or if you have complained about discrimination or supported someone else’s claim.

If you believe you have been treated unfairly at work because of your religion, you can raise the issue with your employer, who should take your complaint seriously and follow a fair procedure to address it. Employers are required to prevent discrimination and bullying and to handle complaints properly. You may choose to take informal or formal steps to resolve the issue, and if necessary, you can seek further advice or make a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Document what happened and gather any evidence of discrimination.  
Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal grievance procedure.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about your options, including making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Harassment at work is unwanted behaviour linked to a protected characteristic that violates your dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment. It can include bullying or harassment by a manager. Discrimination involves treating someone unfairly because of protected characteristics such as age, sex, race, disability, religion, or sexual orientation, among others, under the Equality Act 2010.

If your manager's behaviour is unwanted and related to a protected characteristic, it may be harassment under the law. If it is not linked to a protected characteristic but still involves bullying or harassment, your employer should still take it seriously and address it. You can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer, who has a responsibility to prevent and deal with harassment and discrimination at work.

Step 1: Document the incidents of harassment, noting dates, times, and what happened.  
Step 2: Report the behaviour to your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint.  
Step 3: If your employer does not take appropriate action, you can seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about next steps.

If you think you should have been paid holiday pay but haven’t, you should first check your employment contract and any company policies about holiday pay to understand your entitlement.

If the problem remains unresolved, you may be able to take further action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unpaid wages.

Step 2: Talk to your employer and explain the issue, providing evidence.  
Step 3: If necessary, raise a formal grievance with your employer.

returned_sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you work irregular hours, your holiday pay is calculated based on your average pay over a relevant period, usually the last 52 weeks in which you were paid. This means your employer will look at how much you earned and how many hours you worked during those weeks to work out an average weekly pay. This average is then used to calculate the pay you should receive for your holiday entitlement. For irregular-hours workers, all holiday leave must be paid at their ‘normal’ rate of pay, which includes regular overtime and commission but usually excludes bonuses.

If you have worked less than 52 weeks, your employer will use the average pay for the weeks you have worked. The calculation ensures that your holiday pay fairly reflects your typical earnings, even if your hours and pay vary week to week.

Step 1: Your employer calculates your average weekly pay based on your pay and hours over the last 52 weeks (or fewer if you haven't worked that long).  
Step 2: They multiply this average weekly pay by the number of weeks of holiday you are entitled to (usually 5.6 weeks per year).  
Step 3: You receive holiday pay based on this calculation when you take your holiday.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

retrieved_docs.preview: What is the minimum wage Who gets the minimum wage When increases are paid Checking pay If your employer pays less The real living wage By law, an employer must pay a minimum amount on average for the hours you work. This is called the: Nat…

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers generally cannot include holiday pay in your hourly rate, a practice known as ‘rolled-up holiday pay’. This means your holiday pay should be paid separately from your normal wages. However, there is an exception for workers with irregular hours or part-year workers: for leave years beginning on or before 31 March 2024, employers could use rolled-up holiday pay for these types of workers. After this date, rolled-up holiday pay is no longer allowed for any workers.

If you work irregular hours or part-year, your holiday pay should be calculated based on the hours you have worked rather than being included in your hourly rate. For more detailed guidance or questions about your specific situation, you can contact Acas.

Step 1: Check if you are a worker with irregular hours or part-year employment.
Step 2: Confirm the start date of your leave year (before or after 31 March 2024).
Step 3: If your employer includes holiday pay in your hourly rate and this is after 31 March 2024, you may want to raise the issue with them or seek advice from Acas.

Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if necessary.

The government updates these rates annually, usually in April. You can find the current and upcoming rates on official websites. If your pay is below these rates, your employer may be breaking the law.

You should talk to your employer to raise the issue and show any evidence you have, such as payslips or your contract. If this does not resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer. If your employer still does not pay you correctly, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. Keep in mind there is a time limit for tribunal claims, usually 3 months minus one day from when you should have been paid.

Step 1: Check the current minimum wage rates for your age and status.  
Step 2: Gather evidence like payslips and your employment contract.  
Step 3: Talk to your employer about the issue and ask for the correct pay.  
Step 4: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance with your employer.  
Step 5: Consider legal action such as an employment tribunal claim before the deadline.

- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal for an employer to discriminate against you because of your religion or belief. This protection applies to all aspects of work, including hiring, firing, pay, promotions, and how you are treated day to day. You are also protected if you are associated with someone who has a protected characteristic or if you have made a complaint about discrimination.

If you believe you have been treated unfairly because of your religion, you can raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal grievance procedure. Employers are required to take complaints seriously and should take steps to prevent discrimination and bullying at work. If the issue is not resolved internally, you may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Document what happened and gather any evidence of discrimination.  
Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through their grievance procedure.  
Step 3: If unresolved, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about making a claim.

In the UK, you can be dismissed without notice in certain situations, such as gross misconduct (for example, violence or theft). This is called summary dismissal. However, in most cases, your employer must give you notice of dismissal, either the notice period stated in your contract or the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer. Your employer must have a valid reason for dismissal, act reasonably, and follow a fair procedure.

If you are dismissed, your employer should tell you why you have been dismissed, when your employment ends, and your right to appeal. answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Constructive dismissal happens when an employee resigns because their employer has behaved in a way that fundamentally breaches their contract, making it impossible for the employee to continue working there. This could include things like a significant change to their job duties, not paying them, or creating a hostile work environment. The employee must leave the job because of this breach, not for other reasons.

If an employee believes they have been constructively dismissed, they may be able to claim unfair dismissal. However, it is important that the employee can show the employer's behaviour was serious enough to justify leaving and that they resigned promptly after the breach. Following a fair procedure and seeking advice before resigning can be important steps.

Step 1: Identify the employer’s conduct that breaches the contract.  
Step 2: Consider whether this breach is serious enough to justify resignation.  
Step 3: Resign promptly, making clear it is due to the employer’s breach.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about making a claim.

- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

answer:

In the UK, employers should consult with employees before making them redundant. Consultation is an important part of the redundancy process and allows employees to discuss the reasons for redundancy, explore alternatives, and raise any concerns. For individual redundancies (fewer than 20 employees), there is no fixed minimum consultation period by law, but employers are expected to consult "in good time." For larger redundancies (20 or more employees), there are specific legal consultation periods.

If an employer makes someone redundant without any consultation, this could be unfair dismissal unless there are exceptional circumstances. Employees who believe they have been unfairly dismissed due to redundancy without consultation may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Dismissal is when an employer ends an employee's contract, which is often called being sacked or fired. It can happen for various reasons, such as poor performance, misconduct, or other valid reasons. The employer must follow a fair and reasonable procedure and have a valid reason to dismiss someone.

Redundancy is a specific type of dismissal that happens when an employer needs to reduce their workforce. This could be because the business is closing, the job role is no longer needed, or the employer needs fewer employees to do the work. Redundancy is not about the employee's performance but about the job itself no longer existing.

In summary, all redundancies are dismissals, but not all dismissals are redundancies. Redundancy relates to the job role ending, while dismissal can be for many other reasons.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer dismissed you because you raised a complaint, this could potentially be an unfair dismissal, especially if the complaint was made in good faith and related to your working conditions or rights. Employers must have a valid reason to dismiss someone and must follow a fair and reasonable procedure. Dismissing an employee for raising a complaint may be seen as retaliation, which is generally not a fair reason for dismissal.

You have the right to appeal against the dismissal and your employer should follow a fair procedure during this process. If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal. It is important to act promptly, as there are time limits for making such claims.

Step 1: Check if your dismissal followed a fair procedure and if the reason given was valid.  
Step 2: Appeal the dismissal with your employer if you have not already done so.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a claim for unfair dismissal if you still believe the dismissal was unfair.

retrieved_docs.preview: Dismissal is when your employer ends your employment - they do not always have to give you notice. If you’re dismissed, your employer must show they’ve: a valid reason that they can justify acted reasonably in the circumstances They must al…

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, you can challenge your dismissal at an employment tribunal if you believe it was unfair. For a dismissal to be fair, your employer must have a valid reason, act reasonably in the circumstances, and follow a fair procedure. This includes investigating the situation fully and being consistent in how they treat employees. If you have been employed for at least two years (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012), you have the right to request a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal, which your employer must provide within 14 days.

If you think your dismissal was unfair, you should first appeal the decision with your employer, as they must follow a fair and reasonable procedure. If the appeal does not resolve the issue, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal. However, if you accept a settlement agreement, you usually give up the right to bring an unfair dismissal claim. Keep in mind that there are strict time limits for bringing a tribunal claim, typically within three months minus one day from the date your employment ended.

Step 1: Request a written statement of reasons for your dismissal if you haven't already received one.  
Step 2: Appeal the dismissal with your employer following their procedure.  
Step 3: If the appeal is unsuccessful, consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limit.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor to help with your claim.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals  
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you should first check whether your employer followed a fair and reasonable procedure before dismissing you. Employers must have a valid reason for dismissal and should have given you clear reasons for why you were dismissed, ideally in writing. If you have been employed for at least two years, you have the right to request a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal within 14 days.

You can appeal against the dismissal with your employer, and they should follow a fair procedure during this appeal. If your appeal is successful and you get your job back, this is called a "vanishing dismissal," meaning you cannot make an unfair dismissal claim to an employment tribunal. If you are not satisfied with the outcome, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal, but it is advisable to seek advice before doing so.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you have been employed for two years or more.  
Step 2: Use your employer’s appeal process to challenge the dismissal.  
Step 3: If the appeal is unsuccessful, consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a claim to an employment tribunal.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal for an employer to discriminate against you because of your religion or belief. This protection covers you at work, meaning your employer must not treat you unfairly or less favourably due to your religion. Discrimination can include direct discrimination, indirect discrimination, harassment, or victimisation related to your religion.

If you believe you have been treated unfairly because of your religion, you can raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint or grievance procedure. Employers are required to take such complaints seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination and harassment at work. You may also seek advice or support from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Document what has happened, including dates, times, and details of incidents.
Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal grievance.
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor.

Harassment at work is unwanted behaviour linked to a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010, which violates your dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment. It can include actions or comments related to age, gender reassignment, disability, race, religion, sex, sexual orientation, and other protected characteristics. Harassment can be a form of discrimination if it is connected to one of these protected characteristics.

If your manager's behaviour is unwanted and linked to a protected characteristic, it may be considered harassment under the law. Employers have a responsibility to prevent harassment and take complaints seriously. You can choose to raise the issue informally or formally with your employer, who should investigate and address the situation.

Step 1: Document the incidents of harassment, including dates, times, and details.
Step 2: Report the behaviour to your employer following their grievance procedure or speak to HR.
Step 3: If the employer does not resolve the issue, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about next steps.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Workplace discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 means treating someone unfairly or less favourably because they have a protected characteristic. Protected characteristics include age, gender reassignment, being married or in a civil partnership, pregnancy or maternity, disability, race (including colour, nationality, ethnic or national origin), religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation. Discrimination can happen directly, indirectly, or through harassment or victimisation.

The law protects employees from discrimination at work, and employers must not discriminate against workers based on these characteristics. This protection also extends to people associated with someone who has a protected characteristic or those who have complained about discrimination or supported someone else's claim.

Step 1: Identify if the treatment you or someone else has experienced relates to a protected characteristic.  
Step 2: Consider whether the treatment was unfair or less favourable compared to others without that characteristic.  
Step 3: If you believe discrimination has occurred, you can raise the issue informally with your employer or follow formal complaint procedures. Employers are required to take complaints seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination.

Employers must make reasonable adjustments to accommodate disabled workers to ensure they are not at a disadvantage compared to others.

If you believe you have been treated differently or unfairly because of your disability, you can raise this with your employer or seek advice on how to address the issue. Employers are also encouraged to take positive action to support people with disabilities if they face particular needs or are under-represented.

Step 2: Raise the issue informally with your employer or HR department if you feel comfortable.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Victimisation at work happens when an employer or colleague treats someone badly because they have made a complaint about discrimination or supported someone else’s discrimination claim. It is a form of unfair treatment protected against by the Equality Act 2010. Victimisation can include things like being ignored, given less work, or being unfairly disciplined because of the complaint or support given.

Employers have a responsibility to prevent victimisation and to take any complaints seriously. If you believe you are being victimised, you can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer, and you may have the right to take further action if the problem is not resolved.

Step 1: Keep a record of any incidents of victimisation, including dates and what happened.  
Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint or grievance procedure.  
Step 3: Seek advice from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice if the issue continues or you want to understand your rights better.

answer:

If your employer has ignored your complaint about discrimination, you have several options. First, you can raise the issue informally by talking to your employer or HR department, explaining your concerns clearly and providing any evidence you have. If this does not lead to a resolution, you can make a formal complaint using your employer’s grievance procedure. Employers are legally required to take discrimination complaints seriously and investigate them properly.

If your employer still does not address your complaint, you may consider seeking advice from external organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice. You can also make a claim to an employment tribunal for discrimination under the Equality Act 2010, but it is usually best to try to resolve the issue internally first. Remember, the law protects you from victimisation if you have made or supported a discrimination complaint.

Step 1: Raise your concerns informally with your employer or HR.  
Step 2: If unresolved, submit a formal grievance following your employer’s procedure.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for further support.  
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal if necessary.

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, employers have a legal duty to make reasonable adjustments for employees with disabilities. What counts as "reasonable" depends on factors like the size and resources of the employer and the effectiveness of the adjustment.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, workers are entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks' paid holiday per year, which includes full-time, part-time, and zero-hours contract workers. This statutory entitlement amounts to 28 days for someone working a five-day week, and part-time workers receive a proportional amount based on the hours they work. Holiday entitlement starts accruing from the first day of employment and continues during periods such as probation, sickness, and statutory leave (e.g., maternity or paternity leave). Employers may offer enhanced holiday entitlements beyond the statutory minimum, which should be detailed in the employment contract.

Holiday pay must be calculated fairly and cannot be included as a part of the hourly rate (known as "rolled-up holiday pay") for most workers, except in some cases for irregular-hours or part-year workers if their leave year started before 31 March 2024. When an employee leaves a job, their final pay may include payment for any unused holiday entitlement, and employers should clearly explain how the final pay is calculated, including any deductions or additional payments.

Step 1: Check your employment contract for your holiday entitlement and whether it includes any enhanced holiday beyond the statutory minimum.  
Step 2: Keep track of the holiday you take and any accrued but unused holiday.  
Step 3: If you leave your job, ensure you receive payment for any unused holiday as part of your final pay and ask your employer for a clear breakdown if needed.  
Step 4: Contact ACAS or Citizens Advice if you have questions or disputes about your holiday entitlement or pay.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you work irregular hours, your holiday pay is calculated based on your average pay over a relevant period, usually the last 52 weeks in which you were paid. To work this out, your employer will add up all the pay you received for the hours you worked during those weeks and then divide by the number of weeks worked to find an average weekly pay. This average is then used to calculate the pay you should receive for your holiday entitlement. For irregular-hours workers, all holiday leave must be paid at your ‘normal’ rate of pay, which includes regular overtime and commission but usually excludes bonuses.

If you have worked less than 52 weeks, the calculation will be based on the weeks you have worked. Your employer should use this average pay to ensure you receive the correct holiday pay for the time off you take.

Step 1: Your employer calculates your average weekly pay based on the last 52 weeks you were paid (or fewer if you have not worked that long).  
Step 2: They multiply this average weekly pay by the number of weeks of holiday you take.  
Step 3: You receive holiday pay at this calculated average ‘normal’ rate.

returned_sources.title: What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage
If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers must pay workers at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage depending on their age and status. For example, from 1 April 2026, the National Living Wage for those aged 21 or over is £12.71 per hour. Apprentices and younger workers have different minimum rates. Employers must provide payslips showing how pay is calculated, and they can only make deductions from wages in limited, lawful circumstances.

If you believe your employer has underpaid you or made unlawful deductions, you should first try to resolve the issue by talking to them and showing any evidence you have. If this does not work, you can raise a formal grievance. If the problem persists, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages, but this must be done within three months minus one day from the date the wages were due. Alternatively, you can take a claim to a small claims court. It is important to check your employment status and the minimum wage rates applicable to you.

Step 1: Check your pay against the current minimum wage rates for your age and status.  
Step 2: Gather evidence such as payslips, contracts, and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 3: Talk to your employer to try and resolve the issue informally.  
Step 4: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance with your employer.  
Step 5: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal or small claims court if necessary, within the time limits.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/pay

returned_sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has not paid your wages on the agreed payday, you should first talk to them to find out why. It may be a mistake that they can quickly fix by paying you what you are owed straight away. If talking to your employer does not resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance with them if they have a grievance procedure. Keep any evidence you have, such as payslips or contracts, to support your claim.

If the problem continues and your employer still does not pay you, you may be able to take legal action. You can make a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages, but you must do this within 3 months minus one day from the date you should have been paid. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim unpaid wages through a small claims court instead. It is usually best to try to resolve the issue without legal action if you plan to continue working for your employer.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department about the missing wages.  
Step 2: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance if possible.  
Step 3: Consider seeking advice from a trade union representative or an adviser.  
Step 4: If necessary, make a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limit or use the small claims court.

returned_sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can only deduct money from your wages if it is allowed by your contract or if you have agreed in writing. For example, deductions can be made for damaged property, missing money from the till, or the cost of a uniform, but only if these are specified in your contract or you have given written consent. Additionally, if deductions are for missing money or damaged stock, your employer must follow extra rules: they can only deduct up to 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, must start deductions within 12 months of noticing the issue, and must provide you with written details of each deduction.

If your employer has deducted money without explaining why or without your agreement, you should first check your contract and any written agreements to see if such deductions are allowed. If you are unsure or believe the deduction is unfair, you should seek advice from an adviser or a relevant organisation.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Ask your employer to explain the deduction and provide written details.  
Step 3: If you disagree or don’t receive an explanation, seek advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser.

- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you think you are being paid below the National Minimum Wage (NMW) or National Living Wage (NLW), you should first check the current rates for your age group to confirm. Employers must pay at least the minimum wage for your age or apprenticeship status. If your pay is below this, your employer may be breaking the law. You can talk to your employer or payroll department to raise the issue and ask for the correct pay. If you are a member of a trade union, you can also ask your representative for help.

If talking to your employer does not resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with them. If the issue remains unresolved, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. Keep any evidence you have, such as payslips or your employment contract, to support your claim. Remember, the deadline to start an employment tribunal claim is usually 3 months minus one day from when you should have been paid.

Step 1: Check the current minimum wage rates for your age or apprenticeship status.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer or payroll department about your pay concerns.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if the issue is not resolved informally.  
Step 4: Consider legal action such as an employment tribunal claim if necessary, keeping track of deadlines.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer pays you late every month, you should first talk to them to find out why your pay is delayed and explain how it affects you. It’s important to keep a record of when you were supposed to be paid and when you actually received your wages. If your employer acknowledges the mistake, ask them to pay you as soon as possible and on time in the future.

If talking to your employer doesn’t resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance using your employer’s grievance procedure if they have one. Continuing to work while trying to resolve the problem is usually advisable, as refusing to work might breach your contract. If the problem persists and you are not paid what you are owed, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. However, this should be a last resort, and there are strict time limits for making such claims (usually within 3 months minus one day from the date the payment was due).

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department to understand the reason for late payments.  
Step 2: Keep records of pay dates and amounts.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if necessary.  
Step 4: Consider legal action if the issue is not resolved, keeping in mind the time limits for claims.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can make deductions from your wages only if it is allowed by your contract or you have agreed in writing. Common reasons for deductions include overpayments that you need to pay back, damaged property, missing money from the till, or the cost of a uniform. However, there are rules your employer must follow, especially for deductions related to missing money or damaged stock: they must have a written agreement, not deduct more than 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, start deductions within 12 months of noticing the issue, and provide you with written details of each deduction.

If you think your employer has made an unlawful deduction, you should first check your contract and any agreements you have. If you disagree with the deduction, you should talk to your employer or seek advice from an adviser. If the deduction brings your pay below the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage, this could be illegal.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer to clarify or dispute the deduction.  
Step 3: Seek advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser if needed.  
Step 4: If unresolved, consider raising a formal grievance or taking further action.

What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, whether your employer must pay you for overtime depends on your contract and the terms agreed. There is no automatic legal right to overtime pay unless your contract or workplace policy states otherwise. However, your employer must pay you at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for all hours worked, including overtime. If your total pay divided by the total hours worked (including overtime) falls below the minimum wage, your employer is not paying you legally.

If you believe you are owed pay for overtime, check your contract and payslips to see what you agreed about overtime pay. If your employer has not paid you what you are owed, you should raise the issue with them first. If this does not resolve the problem, you may be able to make a claim for unpaid wages through an employment tribunal or small claims court, but there are time limits for doing this.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and payslips to confirm your entitlement to overtime pay.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer or payroll department to raise the issue.  
Step 3: If unresolved, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified adviser about making a claim for unpaid wages.

returned_sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, your payslip should clearly show your pay and any deductions made from it. This includes your gross pay (the total amount before deductions), the amount of tax and National Insurance deducted, and any other deductions such as pension contributions or student loan repayments. It should also show your net pay (the amount you take home after deductions). Your payslip should be provided on or before your payday.

Payslips help you check that you have been paid correctly and understand how your pay is calculated. If you think there is a mistake or an unlawful deduction, you should first check your contract and any written agreements. If you are unsure, you can seek advice from an employment adviser.

Step 1: Check your payslip each payday to ensure it shows your gross pay, all deductions, and net pay.  
Step 2: Compare the payslip details with your contract or agreement to confirm deductions are lawful.  
Step 3: If you spot any errors or deductions you do not agree with, raise the issue with your employer or payroll department.  
Step 4: If the issue is not resolved, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer should pay your final pay on the date they normally pay you, even if you leave partway through a pay period. This final pay may include outstanding wages, holiday pay, redundancy pay, or deductions for things like training courses. Employers must clearly explain how your final pay is calculated, and your payslip should show any payments or deductions. If you do not receive your final payslip or final pay on time, you should contact your employer to ask for clarification.

If your employer withholds your final pay without a valid reason, you can raise the issue with them directly. If this does not resolve the problem, you may be able to make a claim for unpaid wages through an employment tribunal or small claims court, but there are strict time limits for doing so (usually within 3 months minus a day from when you should have been paid). It is advisable to seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice if you face difficulties.

Step 1: Check your final payslip and payment date with your employer.  
Step 2: Ask your employer for an explanation if your final pay is missing or incorrect.  
Step 3: Contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance if the issue is not resolved.  
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal or small claims court if necessary, keeping in mind the time limits.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can require you to do training, but they cannot use mandatory training as a reason not to pay you at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for the time spent training. Even if your contract says you won’t be paid for mandatory training, your average hourly pay must still meet the legal minimum wage requirements. If you are being charged or deducted money for training, this could be an unlawful deduction from your wages.

If you believe your employer is not paying you correctly for training time or is charging you unfairly, you should check your employment contract and any company policies related to training and pay. You may also want to gather evidence such as payslips and contracts to support your case.

Step 1: Review your employment contract and any training policies.  
Step 2: Check if your pay meets or exceeds the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for training hours.  
Step 3: If you think you are owed pay or have been unfairly charged, contact HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) or Citizens Advice for guidance on how to proceed.

For example, they cannot deduct more than 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, must start deductions within 12 months of noticing the overpayment, and must provide you with written details of each deduction.

If you do not agree that you were overpaid or cannot afford the deductions, you should explain why to your employer and ask them to pay you what you believe you are owed. You can also request smaller deductions spread over a longer period if you are struggling to repay the amount. If you are unsure about your rights or how to handle the situation, it is advisable to seek advice from an employment adviser.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements to see if deductions for overpayments are allowed.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer to clarify the overpayment and discuss repayment options.  
Step 3: If you disagree or need help, contact an adviser for guidance.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

If they make changes without telling you or without your consent, this could be a breach of your contract.

If you believe your pay has been unlawfully deducted or reduced, you can seek advice and potentially take legal action to recover the correct amount.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and payslips for details about your pay.  
Step 2: Speak to your employer or payroll department to ask about any changes.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer hasn’t paid you for work you have already done, you should first talk to them to find out why. Check your payslip if you have one to understand the issue better. If it was a mistake, ask your employer to pay you straight away and not wait until your next pay day. If talking to your employer doesn’t solve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance if your employer has a grievance procedure.

If the issue remains unresolved, you may consider taking legal action. You can make a claim to an employment tribunal for unpaid wages or unlawful deductions, but you must do this within 3 months minus one day from when you should have been paid. If you miss this deadline, you might still be able to claim through a small claims court. It’s usually best to try to resolve the issue directly with your employer before taking legal steps.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department to understand why you haven’t been paid.  
Step 2: If necessary, raise a formal grievance following your employer’s procedure.  
Step 3: If unresolved, consider legal action by contacting an employment tribunal or small claims court before the deadline.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Dismissal happens when an employer ends an employee’s contract, which is often called being sacked or fired. For a dismissal to be fair, the employer must have a valid reason, act reasonably, and follow a fair and consistent procedure, usually in line with the Acas Code of Practice. The employer should investigate the situation fully before dismissing someone and treat all employees equally in similar circumstances.

If you are dismissed, your employer must tell you why you have been dismissed, when your contract ends, your notice period, and your right to appeal. You have the right to ask for a written statement of the reasons for dismissal if you have been employed for at least two years (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012). Employers must provide this within 14 days of your request. Special rules apply if you are dismissed while on maternity leave, where the employer must give you the reasons in writing regardless of your length of service.

If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you can appeal the decision internally. If the employer does not follow a fair procedure, you may be able to bring a claim for unfair dismissal to an employment tribunal. However, if you successfully appeal and get your job back, this is called a "vanishing dismissal," and you cannot make an unfair dismissal claim. Settlement agreements can also be used to end employment by mutual consent, but signing one usually means you cannot bring an unfair dismissal claim.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written explanation of your dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 2: Consider appealing the dismissal if you think it was unfair.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice if you want to understand your rights or consider making a claim.

- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

If you are dismissed, your employer must have a valid reason and act reasonably in the circumstances. They should also follow a fair procedure before dismissing you. answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Constructive dismissal happens when an employee resigns because their employer has behaved in a way that seriously breaches their contract, making it impossible for the employee to continue working there. This could include things like a significant change to job duties, not paying wages, or creating a hostile work environment. The employee must leave the job because of the employer’s conduct, not for other reasons.

If an employee believes they have been constructively dismissed, they may be able to claim unfair dismissal at an employment tribunal. It is important that the employee can show the employer’s behaviour was a fundamental breach of contract and that they resigned soon after the breach occurred.

Step 1: Consider if the employer’s actions amount to a serious breach of contract.  
Step 2: If so, resign promptly and clearly state that you are resigning because of the employer’s conduct.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about making a claim for unfair dismissal.

Employers must follow a fair and reasonable procedure when dismissing someone, including giving reasons for the dismissal and allowing the employee to appeal.

Redundancy is a specific type of dismissal that happens when an employer needs to reduce their workforce. This could be because the job is no longer needed, the business is closing, or the employer needs fewer employees to do the work. Redundancy is different from other dismissals because it is not about the employee's conduct or performance but about the role itself being eliminated.

Step 1: Understand that dismissal is a broad term for ending employment, while redundancy is a specific reason related to the job no longer existing.  
Step 2: Check if the employer has followed a fair procedure and given proper notice and reasons.  
Step 3: If you think the dismissal or redundancy was unfair, you can appeal or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

answer:

You can challenge your dismissal at an employment tribunal if you believe it was unfair. For a dismissal to be fair, your employer must have a valid reason, act reasonably, and follow a fair procedure. This includes investigating the situation fully and being consistent in how they treat employees. If you have been employed for at least two years (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012), you have the right to request a written statement from your employer explaining the reasons for your dismissal, which they must provide within 14 days.

If you think your dismissal was unfair, you should first consider appealing the decision with your employer. If the appeal is unsuccessful, you may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal. However, if you accept a settlement agreement, you usually give up the right to make an unfair dismissal claim. It is important to act quickly because there are strict time limits for making tribunal claims.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement of reasons for dismissal if you haven’t already received one.  
Step 2: Consider appealing the dismissal internally with your employer.  
Step 3: If the appeal fails, seek advice and consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limit.
