returned_sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, workers are entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks' paid holiday per year, which can include bank holidays. This entitlement applies whether you work full-time, part-time, or under a zero-hours contract. If you work part-time, your holiday entitlement is calculated proportionally to the hours you work to ensure you are not treated less favourably than full-time workers. If your employment lasts less than a full year, you are entitled to a proportionate amount of holiday based on the time you have worked.

Holiday entitlement starts accruing from your first day of work and continues even if you are on probation, sick leave, or statutory leave such as maternity or paternity leave. Some employers may offer enhanced or contractual holiday entitlement above the statutory minimum, which should be detailed in your contract.

Step 1: Check your employment contract to see your holiday entitlement and whether it includes bank holidays or any enhanced holiday.  
Step 2: Calculate your holiday entitlement based on your working hours and length of employment if you have worked less than a year.  
Step 3: Keep a record of the holidays you take and discuss with your employer if you believe your entitlement is not being met.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Workers in the UK are entitled to 5.6 weeks of paid holiday per year, which includes public holidays. Holiday pay is calculated based on the worker’s average weekly pay, considering the last 52 weeks worked. For workers with irregular hours or part-year contracts, the calculation uses their normal rate of pay, which includes regular overtime and commission but usually excludes bonuses. Employers must pay at least four weeks of holiday at the worker’s normal rate and the remaining 1.6 weeks at a basic rate, except for irregular-hours workers, who receive all holiday pay at their normal rate.

If you are unsure about your holiday entitlement or how your holiday pay is calculated, you can check your contract or speak to your employer. If disputes arise, you may consider raising the issue informally first, then formally if needed.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and pay slips to understand your holiday entitlement and pay calculation.  
Step 2: Speak to your employer or HR department if you believe your holiday pay is incorrect.  
Step 3: If unresolved, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for further support.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your employer has wronged you, such as by not paying wages owed, discriminating against you, or unfairly dismissing you, there are steps you can take to resolve the issue. First, try to resolve the matter informally by talking to your employer and explaining the problem clearly, providing any evidence you have. If this does not work, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer following their grievance procedure if they have one.

If the grievance does not resolve the issue, you may consider taking legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal. Before doing so, it is important to gather all relevant evidence, understand your employment status, and be aware of deadlines for making claims. For dismissal issues, note that settlement agreements may prevent you from making a claim if you sign them. It is advisable to seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor before proceeding.

Step 1: Talk to your employer informally about the issue, presenting any evidence.
Step 2: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance using your employer’s procedure.
Step 3: If still unresolved, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a solicitor.
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the legal time limits.
Step 5: Prepare your case with evidence and follow tribunal procedures.

- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your employer has not paid you what they owe, you should first check your contract and any written agreements to see if deductions are allowed. Employers can only make deductions if they have your written agreement or if it is stated in your contract. There are specific rules for deductions related to overpayments, damaged property, or missing money, including limits on how much can be deducted and time limits for starting deductions. If you disagree with a deduction, you should raise this with your employer and seek advice if needed.

For unpaid wages or unlawful deductions, you can get advice from the Acas helpline or Citizens Advice. They can help you understand your rights and how to make a complaint if necessary. If your complaint relates to the National Minimum Wage, HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) can also be contacted.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Discuss any disputed deductions with your employer.  
Step 3: Contact Acas or Citizens Advice for free, confidential advice.  
Step 4: If unresolved, consider making a formal complaint to the appropriate authority.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/pay-and-work-rights
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you want to write an email to your employer about unpaid wages and mention the possibility of an employment tribunal, it’s important to be clear, polite, and factual. You should explain the issue, state the amount owed, and give them a reasonable deadline to pay. You can also mention that if the issue is not resolved, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal. It’s best to keep the tone professional and avoid aggressive language.

Step 1: Start by explaining the problem clearly, including the dates and amounts unpaid.  
Step 2: Ask them to pay the outstanding amount by a specific date.  
Step 3: Mention that if the payment is not made, you may have to consider making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages.  
Step 4: Keep a copy of the email and any responses for your records.

Example:  
"Dear [Employer’s Name],  
I am writing to raise a concern regarding unpaid wages for the period [dates]. According to my records, I am owed £[amount]. I would appreciate it if you could arrange payment by [reasonable deadline]. If I do not receive payment by this date, I may have to consider making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. I hope we can resolve this matter promptly.  
Kind regards,  
[Your Name]"

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

It is against the law in the UK to discriminate against someone because of protected characteristics such as age, gender reassignment, marriage or civil partnership status, pregnancy or maternity, disability, race, religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation. This protection applies in various areas including work, education, and when using public services. The Equality Act 2010 provides this legal protection. You are also protected if you are associated with someone who has a protected characteristic or if you have complained about discrimination or supported someone else’s claim.

If you believe you have been discriminated against at work, you can take action under this law. Employers are also allowed to take positive action to help people with protected characteristics if they face disadvantages or are under-represented in certain roles.

Step 1: Identify if the discrimination relates to a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010.  
Step 2: Gather any evidence or details about the discrimination incident(s).  
Step 3: Consider raising the issue with your employer or HR department.  
Step 4: If unresolved, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Workers in the UK are entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks’ paid holiday each year. This entitlement applies to full-time, part-time, and casual workers. Holiday pay is calculated based on the worker’s average weekly pay, which includes regular overtime, commission, and other payments related to length of service or qualifications, but usually excludes bonuses. For workers with regular hours, at least 4 weeks of holiday must be paid at their normal rate, with the remaining 1.6 weeks paid at a basic rate. For irregular-hours workers, all holiday pay should be at their normal rate.

If a worker is off unexpectedly due to sickness or other emergencies, employers should support them and help with a smooth return to work. Workers can take time off for various reasons, including planned leave like holidays or longer-term leave such as parental leave.

Step 1: Check your contract or company policy for specific holiday entitlement details.  
Step 2: Calculate your average weekly pay based on your hours and pay over the last 52 weeks.  
Step 3: Confirm with your employer how holiday pay is calculated and paid.  
Step 4: If you believe your holiday pay or entitlement is incorrect, raise the issue informally with your employer first. If unresolved, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

answer:

I can't act as your solicitor or give you legal advice on what to do next to win a case. However, I can provide general information about your rights and possible steps you might consider depending on your issue. For example, if your concern is about unpaid wages, you should first talk to your employer to understand why you haven't been paid and ask for the money owed. If that doesn't work, you may be able to take legal action. If your issue involves discrimination, the Equality Act 2010 protects you from unfair treatment based on protected characteristics. For dismissal concerns, understanding whether your dismissal was fair or unfair is important, and settlement agreements may be involved. For holiday entitlement, you can check your entitlement and discuss it with your employer or HR.

Step 1: Identify the specific issue you are facing (e.g., unpaid wages, discrimination, unfair dismissal, holiday pay).  
Step 2: Gather any relevant documents such as payslips, contracts, or correspondence.  
Step 3: Try to resolve the matter informally by speaking with your employer or HR department.  
Step 4: If informal resolution fails, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor who can provide tailored legal advice.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your employer has treated you unfairly, such as not paying wages owed, discriminating against you, or unfairly dismissing you, you can take steps to resolve the issue. First, try to resolve the problem informally by talking to your employer and explaining the issue clearly with any evidence you have. If this does not work, you can raise a formal grievance following your employer’s grievance procedure. If the grievance does not resolve the matter, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Here is a general step-by-step plan:
Step 1: Gather all relevant evidence, such as your contract, payslips, emails, or notes about incidents.
Step 2: Raise the issue informally with your employer, explaining clearly what you believe is wrong and what you want.
Step 3: If informal talks fail, submit a formal grievance in writing, following your employer’s grievance procedure.
Step 4: If the grievance is not resolved, consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the legal deadline (usually three months less one day from the event).
Step 5: Prepare your case for the tribunal, including all evidence and a clear explanation of what you want.
Step 6: Attend the tribunal hearing and present your case.

What you say will depend on your specific issue, but always be clear, factual, and polite. For example, if unpaid wages are the issue, explain the amount owed and provide evidence like payslips or contracts. If discrimination or unfair dismissal is involved, describe what happened and how it breaches your rights under the Equality Act 2010 or employment law.

If you are unsure about your employment status or the best course of action, you can seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor.

- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).
If you believe your employer has not paid you what they owe, you should first check your contract and any written agreements to understand if deductions are allowed. Employers can only make deductions if they have your agreement in writing or if it is stated in your contract. There are specific rules about deductions for things like damaged property or missing money, including limits on how much can be deducted and time limits on when deductions can start.

If you disagree with a deduction or think you have not been paid correctly, you should raise the issue with your employer and explain why you believe you are owed money. If you cannot resolve the issue directly, you can seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice, who provide free and confidential guidance on pay disputes and employment rights.

Step 1: Review your contract and any written agreements about pay and deductions.  
Step 2: Discuss the issue with your employer, providing reasons why you believe you are owed money.  
Step 3: If unresolved, contact ACAS helpline or Citizens Advice for further support and advice.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you want to write an email to your employer about unpaid wages and mention the possibility of an employment tribunal, it’s important to be clear, polite, and factual. Start by explaining the issue, including how much you believe you are owed and for what period. Mention that you have tried to resolve the matter informally and that you are requesting payment by a specific date. You can then state that if the issue is not resolved, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal, which has a strict time limit of 3 months minus one day from when the payment was due. It’s also helpful to mention that you prefer to resolve the matter without legal action.

Step 1: Clearly state the amount owed and the period it covers.  
Step 2: Explain that you have tried to resolve the issue informally.  
Step 3: Request payment by a specific deadline.  
Step 4: Politely inform them that if payment is not made, you may take the matter to an employment tribunal within the legal time limit.  
Step 5: Express your preference to settle the matter amicably.

returned_sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, workers are entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks' paid holiday per year, which is called statutory annual leave. This entitlement applies whether you work full time, part time, or under a zero-hours contract. For part-time workers, the holiday entitlement is proportional to the hours worked, ensuring they are not treated less favourably than full-time workers. If you work irregular hours or part of the year, there are specific rules to calculate your holiday entitlement fairly.

Holiday entitlement starts accruing from your first day of work and continues even if you are on probation, sick leave, or statutory leave such as maternity or paternity leave. Some employers may offer more than the statutory minimum, known as enhanced or contractual holiday, which will be detailed in your contract. Bank holidays may be included in your total holiday entitlement, so it is important to check your contract for details.

Step 1: Check your employment contract to see your holiday entitlement and whether bank holidays are included.  
Step 2: Calculate your accrued holiday based on your working pattern and length of employment.  
Step 3: If you have questions or disputes about your holiday entitlement or pay, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Workers in the UK are entitled to at least 5.6 weeks of paid holiday per year. Holiday pay is calculated based on the worker’s average weekly pay, which includes regular overtime, commission, and other payments related to length of service or qualifications, but usually excludes bonuses. For workers with regular hours, at least 4 weeks of holiday must be paid at their normal rate, with the remaining 1.6 weeks paid at a basic rate. For irregular-hours workers or those working part of the year, all holiday pay should be at their normal rate.

If a worker is paid monthly, their average hourly pay is calculated by dividing their monthly pay by the hours worked that month, then multiplied by the hours worked in a week to find weekly pay. This average is then used to calculate holiday pay fairly over the relevant period.

Step 1: Calculate the worker’s average weekly pay based on their pay pattern and hours worked.  
Step 2: Multiply the average weekly pay by the number of weeks of holiday entitlement (usually 5.6 weeks).  
Step 3: Ensure the holiday pay reflects the worker’s normal earnings, including regular overtime and commission where applicable.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

I understand you want a step-by-step plan to take legal action against your employer. While I cannot provide advice on how to "win" a case, I can outline general steps you might consider if you believe your employer has treated you unfairly, such as not paying wages owed or discrimination.

Step 1: Gather evidence. Collect all relevant documents such as your employment contract, payslips, emails, or any written agreements. This will support your claim.

Step 2: Raise the issue informally. Talk to your employer or HR department to explain the problem clearly and calmly, showing your evidence.

Step 3: Use formal procedures. If informal talks fail, check if your employer has a grievance procedure and raise a formal complaint.

Step 4: Seek advice. Contact organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance on your situation.

Step 5: Consider legal action. If the issue remains unresolved, you may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal, for example for unpaid wages or discrimination. Be aware there are strict time limits for making claims.

Step 6: Prepare your case. If you proceed, gather all evidence and consider getting legal advice or representation.

If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your employer has not paid you what they owe, you should first check your contract and any written agreements to understand if deductions are allowed. Employers can only deduct money if it is agreed in your contract or you have given written permission. For example, deductions for damaged property or missing money from the till must follow strict rules, including limits on the amount and timing of deductions. If you disagree with a deduction or think your employer has taken money unlawfully, you should raise the issue with your employer and seek advice from a trusted source.

If you want to calculate your holiday pay, workers in the UK are entitled to 5.6 weeks of paid holiday per year. Holiday pay is calculated based on your average weekly pay, which includes regular overtime and commission but usually excludes bonuses. For workers paid monthly, the calculation involves averaging hourly pay over the last month and then multiplying by the hours worked in a week. Employers must pay at least 4 weeks of holiday at your normal rate and the remaining 1.6 weeks at a basic rate.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and pay slips to understand your pay and any deductions.  
Step 2: Calculate your holiday pay entitlement using your average weekly pay over the relevant period.  
Step 3: If you believe you have not been paid correctly or deductions are unfair, raise the issue with your employer.  
Step 4: Contact the Acas helpline or Citizens Advice for free, confidential advice on your rights and next steps.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/pay-and-work-rights
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you want to write an email to your employer about unpaid wages and mention the possibility of an employment tribunal, it’s important to be clear, polite, and factual. Start by explaining the issue, state the amount owed, and ask for payment by a specific date. You can mention that if the issue is not resolved, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal as a last resort. Keep the tone professional and avoid aggressive language.

Step 1: Clearly state the amount of unpaid wages and the period they cover.  
Step 2: Request payment by a reasonable deadline (for example, within 14 days).  
Step 3: Mention that if payment is not made, you may consider taking the matter to an employment tribunal, but that you prefer to resolve it informally.  
Step 4: Keep a copy of the email and any responses as evidence.

Example wording:  
"Dear [Employer’s Name],  
I am writing to raise a concern regarding unpaid wages amounting to £[amount], which were due on [date(s)]. I would appreciate it if you could arrange payment by [date]. If this issue is not resolved by then, I may have to consider making a claim to an employment tribunal to recover the money owed. However, I hope we can resolve this matter without the need for formal action.  
Please let me know if you need any further information from me.  
Kind regards,  
[Your Name]"

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

It is against the law to discriminate against someone at work because of protected characteristics such as age, gender reassignment, pregnancy, disability, race, religion, sex, or sexual orientation, among others. This protection comes from the Equality Act 2010. You are also protected if you are associated with someone who has a protected characteristic or if you have complained about discrimination or supported someone else’s claim.

If you believe you have been discriminated against, you can take action under the Equality Act 2010. Employers can also take positive action to help people with protected characteristics if they are disadvantaged or under-represented.

Step 1: Identify if the discrimination relates to a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010.  
Step 2: Gather any evidence of the discrimination or unfair treatment.  
Step 3: Consider raising the issue with your employer or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for next steps.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Workers in the UK are entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks’ paid holiday each year. Holiday pay is calculated based on the worker’s average weekly pay, which includes regular overtime, commission, and other consistent payments but usually excludes bonuses. For workers with regular hours, at least 4 weeks of holiday must be paid at their normal rate of pay, with the remaining 1.6 weeks paid at a basic rate. For workers with irregular hours or those who work part of the year, all holiday pay should be at their normal rate.

To calculate holiday pay, employers typically use the average pay over the last 52 weeks in which the worker was paid, or fewer weeks if the worker has not been employed that long. For monthly-paid workers, the average hourly pay is calculated for the last month and then multiplied by the number of hours worked in a typical week to find the weekly pay.

Step 1: Check your contract or employer’s holiday policy to confirm your entitlement.  
Step 2: Calculate your average weekly pay based on your recent earnings and hours worked.  
Step 3: Ensure your employer pays holiday pay at the correct rate according to your working pattern.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your employer has treated you unfairly, such as not paying wages owed, discriminating against you, or unfairly dismissing you, there are steps you can take to resolve the issue. First, try to resolve the matter informally by talking to your employer and explaining the problem clearly, providing any evidence you have. If this does not work, you can raise a formal grievance following your employer’s grievance procedure.

If the grievance does not resolve the issue, you may consider taking legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal. Before doing so, it is important to gather all relevant evidence, including your contract, pay slips, and any correspondence. Note that settlement agreements can be used to end disputes but usually prevent you from making further claims. For specific advice on what to say and when, or to understand your chances of success, you should consult a qualified solicitor, ACAS, or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Talk to your employer informally about the issue, showing any evidence.
Step 2: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance using your employer’s procedure.
Step 3: Gather all relevant documents and evidence related to your claim.
Step 4: Consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor.
Step 5: If advised, submit a claim to an employment tribunal within the required deadlines.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your employer has not paid you what they owe, you should first check your contract and any written agreements to understand if deductions are allowed. Employers can only deduct money for specific reasons, such as overpayments or damage, if this is agreed in writing or stated in your contract. There are also limits on deductions, for example, they cannot take more than 10% of your pay before tax on each pay day for certain types of deductions, and they must provide written details each time.

If you disagree with a deduction or think your employer has deducted pay unlawfully, you should raise the issue with your employer and explain why you believe the deduction is incorrect. If you cannot resolve the issue, you can seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice, who provide free and confidential help on pay disputes.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Discuss the issue with your employer, providing reasons why you think the deduction is wrong.  
Step 3: If unresolved, contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for further guidance and support.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/pay-and-work-rights
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you want to write an email to your employer threatening to take your claim to an employment tribunal unless they pay you what you are owed, it is important to keep the tone professional and factual. You should clearly state the amount you believe you are owed, reference your attempts to resolve the issue informally, and mention the legal right to make a claim if the matter is not resolved. You can also mention the time limit for making a tribunal claim, which is usually 3 months minus one day from when the payment was due.

Here is a simple example of what you might write:

---

Subject: Request for Payment of Outstanding Wages

Dear [Employer's Name],

I am writing to formally request payment of the outstanding wages owed to me for the period [specify period]. Despite previous discussions, I have not yet received this payment, which I believe is in breach of my employment contract and the National Minimum Wage requirements.

Please be aware that if this matter is not resolved promptly, I may have to consider making a claim to an employment tribunal. The deadline for such a claim is usually 3 months minus one day from the date the payment was due.

I would prefer to resolve this matter amicably and look forward to your prompt response.

Kind regards,  
[Your Name]

---

Step 1: Gather evidence of the unpaid wages (pay slips, contract, correspondence).  
Step 2: Send a clear, polite email as above.  
Step 3: If no response or payment is made, consider raising a formal grievance with your employer.  
Step 4: If still unresolved, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limit.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Being dismissed via social media is generally not considered a fair or appropriate way for an employer to end your employment. A dismissal means your employer ends your contract, and they should follow a fair and reasonable procedure, including giving you clear reasons for the dismissal, notice period, and the right to appeal. Employers are also expected to respect confidentiality and communicate dismissal in a professional manner.

If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you can appeal the decision with your employer. If the employer does not follow a fair procedure, you may have grounds to make a claim for unfair dismissal to an employment tribunal, provided you meet the qualifying criteria such as length of service. It is advisable to request a written explanation for your dismissal from your employer within 14 days.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal.  
Step 2: Consider appealing the dismissal if you believe it was unfair or handled improperly.  
Step 3: If the appeal is unsuccessful and you believe the dismissal was unfair, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for support and guidance on your specific situation.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

retrieved_docs.preview: What dismissal is Types of dismissal Dismissals with and without notice Unfair dismissal Constructive dismissal Following a fair procedure A dismissal is when an employer ends an employee's contract. It usually means the same as being sacke…

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, an employer usually must give notice before dismissing an employee. The notice period must be at least the length stated in the employee's contract or the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer. However, there are exceptions where an employer can dismiss an employee immediately without notice, such as in cases of gross misconduct like violence.

If you are dismissed, your employer should tell you why you are being dismissed, when your employment will end, and your right to appeal. For employees with at least two years' service (or one year if started before 6 April 2012), you have the right to ask for a written statement explaining the reasons for dismissal, which your employer must provide within 14 days.

Step 1: Check your employment contract for your notice period.  
Step 2: If you are dismissed, ask your employer for a written explanation if you have the required length of service.  
Step 3: If you believe your dismissal was unfair or notice was not given properly, consider raising a grievance or seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

Step 1: Identify the specific employment issue you need help with (e.g., unfair dismissal, discrimination, pay, holiday entitlement).  
Step 2: Contact Citizens Advice Scotland or a qualified solicitor who specialises in Scottish employment law.  
Step 3: Use official Scottish government or ACAS Scotland resources for up-to-date and relevant information.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Losing your job can happen for various reasons, including dismissal, redundancy, or resignation. If you believe you were unfairly dismissed, you may have rights under UK employment law, but usually, you need to have worked for your employer for a certain period before making a claim. If you signed a settlement agreement when leaving, you generally cannot bring an unfair dismissal claim.

If you want to understand more about your dismissal or consider your options, you might want to contact an adviser or the Acas helpline for guidance. They can help you understand whether your dismissal was fair and what steps you can take next.

Step 1: Check if you were given a reason for your dismissal and whether any disciplinary or redundancy procedures were followed.  
Step 2: Consider whether you signed any settlement agreement when leaving.  
Step 3: Contact Acas or Citizens Advice for advice on your specific situation.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe you are being discriminated against at work, you are protected by the Equality Act 2010. Discrimination can be direct or indirect and may include harassment or victimisation. You can choose how to respond, either informally or formally. Employers have a responsibility to prevent discrimination and should take any complaints seriously.

You can start by raising the issue with your employer, either informally by talking to your manager or HR, or formally by following your employer’s grievance procedure. If you witness discrimination against someone else, you can also report it. Employers should handle complaints properly and take steps to stop discrimination happening.

Step 1: Consider talking informally to your employer or HR about the discrimination.  
Step 2: If informal talks don’t help, raise a formal grievance following your employer’s procedure.  
Step 3: Keep records of incidents and any communications about the issue.  
Step 4: If the problem continues, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about next steps, including possible legal claims.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, it is illegal to discriminate against someone because of certain protected characteristics such as age, gender reassignment, marriage or civil partnership status, pregnancy or maternity, disability, race, religion or belief, sex, or sexual orientation. This protection applies in the workplace, education, when using public services, and other areas. If you believe you have been discriminated against at work, you have the right to take action and your employer should take any complaints seriously.

If you experience discrimination, you can choose to raise the issue informally or formally with your employer. Employers are required to prevent discrimination and deal with complaints appropriately. You may also be protected if you have supported someone else's discrimination claim or complained about discrimination yourself.

Step 1: Identify the type of discrimination and gather any evidence you have.
Step 2: Consider raising the issue informally with your employer or HR department.
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, you may make a formal complaint following your employer’s grievance procedure.
Step 4: If necessary, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your next steps.

If you believe you have been discriminated against at work, you are protected by the Equality Act 2010, which makes it unlawful to discriminate based on protected characteristics such as age, disability, race, sex, religion, sexual orientation, and others. You can choose to take informal or formal steps to address the issue. It is important to report the discrimination to your employer, who should take your complaint seriously and take steps to prevent further discrimination.

You may start by raising the issue informally with your employer or HR department. If this does not resolve the problem, you can make a formal complaint through your employer’s grievance procedure. Keep a record of incidents and any communications about the discrimination. If the issue remains unresolved, you may consider seeking advice from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice, or ultimately making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Document the discrimination incidents and gather any evidence.  
Step 2: Raise the issue informally with your employer or HR.  
Step 3: If needed, make a formal complaint using your employer’s grievance procedure.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice if the problem continues.  
Step 5: Consider an employment tribunal claim if all other steps fail.

If your problem relates to unpaid wages or deductions, you should first check your employment contract and gather any evidence you have. Then, try talking to your employer to resolve the issue.
